10_Recovery_and_Deload.md: Reduce sets per exercise by ~40% (e.g., 4 sets → 2-3 sets).
Reduce load ~10-20% OR keep load but add 2-3 RIR (further from failure).
No intensity techniques (drop sets, rest-pause, etc.) during a deload week.
Treating deloads as optional/only for "weak" lifters — everyone needs periodic fatigue dissipation, especially past beginner stages.
Waiting until a client is severely overreached before addressing recovery instead of using proactive scheduled deloads.
Ignoring sleep/stress as primary levers and jumping straight to programming changes when a client complains of poor progress.
Deloading too aggressively (near-complete rest) when a lighter volume/intensity deload would have preserved more training stimulus.
"Deloads are a waste of a training week." — False; they are an investment that enables the next block's progress and reduce injury/burnout risk.

"If you're not sore, you're not recovering from anything, so you don't need a deload." — False; fatigue accumulates systemically (CNS, connective tissue, psychological) independent of muscle soreness.
"Real athletes/bodybuilders never deload." — False; virtually all high-level strength/physique athletes use planned deloads or tapers as a core part of their programming.
